comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's a little past midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is L. Frank Baum, author of The Wizard of Oz and its sequels, and a serious Theosophical occultist who put a lot of magical teachings into his books.

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***





[IMAGE:1]

comments.title: Egregore vs. zeitgeist?
comments.content:
What's the difference between an egregore and a zeitgeist?

Also, a little magic humor:

http://www.kimberlysteelemusic.com/lemon.jpg

replies.title: Re: Egregore vs. zeitgeist?
replies.content:
A zeitgeist is specifically the egregor of a given period of time. Think of "the Sixties" as a phenomenon; the distinctive character and energy of that era is its zeitgeist. A zeitgeist remains a living egregor for as long as people who witnessed it are still alive; when I was a kid, the 1890s still had a living egregor, but that's long since faded out.

As for the cartoon, funny. When life gives you demons...

replies.title: Re: Egregore vs. zeitgeist?
replies.content: Fascinating.  Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Egregore vs. zeitgeist?
replies.content: Is there a specific way of protecting yourself from the effects of the egregor of an organisation you've been in for a long time, over and above getting yourself out of it and transferring focus to other things?

replies.title: Re: Egregore vs. zeitgeist?
replies.content: Regular banishing rituals are good for this. It can also help to take any items you still have that you received as a member of the organization, and either burn them or give them away to charity, with the intention of breaking the link that connects you to the organization.

replies.title: Re: Egregore vs. zeitgeist?
replies.content: A priest, a minister, a rabbi, and an archdruid walk into a bar...

comments.title: Effects of a magical practice
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
I have had a regular, daily magical practice for about 3 years now and I am finding myself to be much changed for the better. My husband is not studying but I have always thought he is a better Druid than I am. He is very observant and sensitive.

A few night ago he had a dream which seemed magical to me. A spectral woman came to our bed and she reached out to touch me and he raised his hand to her and pushed her away with his energy. Ghosts sometimes do come to me when I am asleep. Is it possible my practice is affecting my husband and opening him to magical experiences?

Max Rogers

replies.title: Re: Effects of a magical practice
replies.content: It's possible, but if your husband's already sensitive he may simply get magical dreams from time to time.

replies.title: Re: Effects of a magical practice
replies.content:
I guess this is a case of seeing the magic that is all around us. I have noticed quite a few such gifts among my friends and relatives.
Thank you for this forum.
Max Rogers

comments.title: data point
comments.content:
JMG,

I have a date point for you. A few years ago I bought the CGD and got through the ovate curriculum in a bit under a year. But a little while in to the Bardic grade my practice fizzled out. A while ago I came across your discussion of your working to prevent unauthorized use of your books. Well some years ago I downloaded a copy of the Druidry Handbook that I came turned up when I was looking for something else, mind you I did already have a purchased print copy, but I thought a digital one would be handy to have to have. Anyway I deleted it. About couple months latter it seems that obstacles to my progresses have lifted and I have been getting the work done with better results then ever.

Also the Cosmic Doctrine discussion and rereading The Well of Galabes has been a great assistance to my return to working the CGD.
Cheers,
Andrew

replies.title: Re: data point
replies.content: Thanks for this. Doesn't surprise me a bit -- and I'm glad that you figured out what to do and are moving ahead now.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I am curious about egregores, and I do not know if you know all of these things or if they are at all knowable, but here goes:

If an egregore for a specific ritual is built up and strengthened by repetitions and many people practicing it, is a ritual that has been done 10,000 times 100 times stronger than one that has been done 100 times? Or is there a point after which more people/more practice does not make a difference?

Do you find that the Hermetic LBRP has a stronger egregore than the SOP?

You have mentioned the importance of doing rituals exactly as written for the benefit of the egregore—I suppose there is a spectrum here, since one person cannot do the same ritual exactly the same way twice, not to mention someone else. If everyone does a ritual in sort of different ways, does it dilute the egregore?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I don't know of any way to measure this in quantitative terms.

2) I wouldn't say "stronger." The two egregors are very different qualitatively, but each gets the job done.

3) Of course there's a spectrum. As to whether the egregor can be weakened by variations, that depends on a range of things. I routinely give two or more different ways of doing any ritual, so that the egregor of that ritual has a little flexibility built into it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This answered a question I had. I was going to ask why the SoP benefited from an egregor when it has to be personalized, which would seem to go against the advice to always do rituals the same way to get advantage of the egregor.

comments.title: Imaginos
comments.content:
JMG,
Most of the major currents of western esotericism have huge bodies of pictorial instructions, allegorical figures, seed images, tracing boards, et cetera, to aid and instruct practitioners.

Revival Druidry seems to be the exception in this. Was there such a body of work that didn't survive into this century, or was there never much to begin with?

That question being posited, I am working on a 3/4 bird's-eye view of the middle pillar and sphere of protection as a single integrated image for my own purposes, according to the power of my understanding.

.:E:.

replies.title: Re: Imaginos
replies.content:
The Druid Revival tradition has some images of this kind:
[IMAGE:0]
That's Hu the Mighty, in case you were wondering, arriving on the Island of Britain in his coracle, with an oar in one hand and a plough in the other. You could meditate on that for a good long while! But most of it's found in old Welsh sources at this point. Druids of the Revival by and large have tended to use natural phenomena directly as their teaching symbols -- an actual tree, say, instead of an image of a tree.

replies.title: Re: Imaginos
replies.content:
There's quite a bit going on in that image...

.:E:.

replies.title: Re: Imaginos
replies.content: Yep. The other coracles in the background, by the way, are the Welsh people, whom Hu traditionally led to Britain from their original home in Deffrobani in the Region of the Summer Stars...

replies.title: Re: Imaginos
replies.content:
Dear Archdruid,

What book is that from?

Kind regards,
Brigyn.

PS: Actually, could you point me to any other works you know like this? I don’t mind if they’re in Welsh or long out of print.

replies.title: Re: Imaginos
replies.content: I don't know; there are many versions of this image in old Welsh writings, and I simply did an image search online for Hu Gadarn. You might try doing a search for "druidism" on Google Books or Archive.org.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: In studying the Druid Magic Handbook, is it necessary to do the wand, cauldron, and crane bag enchantments before the inner grail working? Or is the order flexible?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You should follow the order of the practices as given in the book, for best results.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

When performing the Sphere of Protection in the Grove Ritual, during the calling of the elements in each gate do you visualize the light of that elemental gate flowing into your solar plexus, into the grove or both? I'm just learning the Grove ritual and was uncertain as what I should do.

Thanks so very much for what you are doing on these blogs and in your books. I'll most likely always be a solitary Druid, but I consider you my Archdruid. You'll be pleased to know that I do not use that as a claim to fame ;)

Sincerely,
Haassmasithiam

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Just visualize it flowing into the grove. Your solar plexus center will draw in what you need from the energies you summon.

comments.title: Amulets
comments.content: Last week you responded to a question about dealing with energy drainage from someone terribly afraid to die, affecting those around them.  I made an amulet from your description and I FEEL BETTER!  About half my work is as a comfort therapist (massage therapy) for Hospice, and I see anywhere from 1 to 4 people a week, and lately, have been so so tired.  Not all of these people are afraid of death, but I've come to believe that their BODIES are trying to find energy anyway.  I'm also starting the LBRP, chapter 3 of Ritual Magic, and so glad to be expanding my spiritual practice.  Grateful for your clear writing and chapter assignments, and the recommended reading as well.  Thanks, John!  --Sidney

replies.title: Re: Amulets
replies.content: Delighted to hear it. You're welcome, and thank you.

replies.title: Re: Amulets
replies.content:
FYI from that previous post...

My mother is wearing the amulet, and I am monitoring the situation. So far so good - remote conversations have stopped triggering the drain. Have yet to see how well it works in physical proximity.

replies.title: Re: Amulets
replies.content: That's good to hear.

comments.title: Energies
comments.content:
Good afternoon, JMG! (At least, it's afternoon here in China...)

Groggy? Mead? Lagavulin? Hehehe

Oh yes, questions: in the DMH, you write on page 149:

"The realm of Spirit Within is the home of the Plant Llŷr. In the last age of the world they were ruled by Brân ap Llŷr; their rulers in this age of the world are Manawydan ap Llŷr and his wife Rhiannon ferch Hefeydd Hên. The Plant Llŷr are the archetypal forms of humanity, and their struggles, triumphs, and tragedies echo the fundamental patterns of human existence. They also represent particular energies within the human body that can be encountered and awakened by a variety of magical practices".

Is there anywhere I can read more about these body energies? Is this something that will be in one of your forthcoming books?

Secondly, an odd thing. My father passed away early this year. He would have laughed at all this Druidry I'm getting into, but he was an old-school farm boy, and in his nature there was much of the Druid. Anyway, he's been in my dreams recently, to the extent that I took the advice you gave in an earlier MM, and have been putting cider vinegar in a small cauldron in my bedroom when I sleep. That seemed to work. Last night, I forgot. and I had vivid dreams of him being referred to by name in the OBOD gwersi. I suppose that this is probably all just my subconscious processing everything in the normal way, but I wondered if it perhaps raises any flags with you?

Regarding my question last week, I'm trying to get the rituals memorized before I ask any questions of the entities concerned!

Many thanks, as always.

Bogatyr

replies.title: Re: Energies
replies.content:
1) No, there's nowhere you can read about that. If you want to work with those energies, why, I'm sure I must have written a Druid magic handbook that provides you with the necessary tools of meditation, pathworking, and ritual... ;-)

2) Are you sure you want to chase him off? Ancestral contacts can have things of value to pass on, you know.

replies.title: Re: Energies
replies.content:
1) Haha, fair enough. Just wondered if it was expounded in more detail anywhere.

2) Also fair enough. It's complicated, and not for discussion here.

Thanks for replying.

Bogatyr

comments.title: Plumbers humour
comments.content:
I had my house renovated last year, including the bathroom.
Anyway, I was crawling under the house the other day and noticed that the plumber (or whoever) has glued a picture out of a porno mag to the underside of the bath.
at first I just laughed, but then on second thought the only person who uses the bath is my young daughter, so on second thoughts take it as a little creepy (I'm sure it wasn't meant that way).

Thinking about it, if there's no intentional magical act to it (assuming it's just a joke), surely there's no effect?
Your thoughts?

replies.title: Re: Plumbers humour
replies.content: I know plumbers who do things like that, so it was probably just a joke. If you want to get rid of it, remove the picture and replace it with a photocopy of a page from your daughter's favorite story book.

comments.title: It Is Everywhere, It Is Everything...
comments.content:
JMG,
An observation and a half-question.

After reading the Cosmic Doctrine posts, I have been working at visualizing the rings cosmos, chaos, and pass-not. Just now while practicing my google-fu by chance my sight fell upon an image of the hexagram circled by a dragon crowned and a serpent, each swallowing the tail of the other.

The dragon crowned could be the the ring cosmos and subsequently the serpent the ring chaos, mais oui?

.:E:.

replies.title: Re: It Is Everywhere, It Is Everything...
replies.content:
Ah, that's the famous emblem from Kirchweger's Aurea Catena Homeri:
[IMAGE:0]
You could interpret it that way, but in the original it's actually an emblem of the relations between the solar and telluric forces, in their alchemical forms of niter and salt.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm the "partial psychic vacuum" (or perhaps just a newly fledged non-chump) from last week, with good news to report: that hoodoo bath seems to have done something useful.

As I passed the cloth over my head and neck, I heard a distinct "kachunk!" like a deadbolt unlatching. It was startling and unusual, and I wondered what the cat had gotten into. Nothing, as far as I can tell -- that was just a loud, odd noise of no known source, and it was followed by a slight but distinct change in my emotional state. You know how it can seem very quiet in your house, and then the fridge stops humming and it really is quiet, surprisingly quiet? It was as if there were some struggle that I'd gotten used to, and it dropped away. The effect was immediate and so far it's been lasting.

I'll be keeping that up. Thanks very much for the advice!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Excellent. Congratulations on your departure from chumphood. ;-)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Will the Dolmen Arch book be structured more like DMH or CGD/LRM?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Very much more like Learning Ritual Magic or The Celtic Golden Dawn. It consists of seven grades, beginning with the Gradd y Newyddian (Grade of the Novice) and ending with the Gradd y Gwyddon Rhydd (Grade of the Free Loremaster), and each grade has a set of papers to study, covering various elements of Druid magic, philosophy, and symbolism.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Hello, JMG, I have a question. In the book "Through the Gates of Death", Dion Fortune mentioned that a person who resists the Second Death out of fear can become "earthbound". Does she mean by that descriptopn vampirism or something else?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's usually nothing so drastic as vampirism. Some ghosts are souls of dead people that have been temporarily trapped in the material world, rather than going on through the afterlife process. Eventually, with or without help, they get over it, but it's not a pleasant or useful state to be stuck in, and some living people make it their business to help ghosts of this kind move on.

comments.title: Folk Magic
comments.content: I wonder if you can advise me on some specific folk magic. I'm setting up my herbal medicine practice in a new area and am looking to attract clients. I'm doing all the obvious marketing things but wondered if there is a bit of folk magic you could recommend to boost things. I've looked in your 'Encyclopaedia of Natural magic' but can't find anything quite right.

replies.title: Re: Folk Magic
replies.content: You can find some spells of that kind here.

comments.title: Gods embodied in lakes and rivers
comments.content:
Do you think the Danube is the body of a god? I ask this based on a couple of comments on Lake Superior in a previous Magic Monday.

I am a former atheist, now agnostic, reading through your Druidry Handbook for the first time. When I read the comments about Lake Superior, something finally clicked. I had not wanted to try anything in the DH, but I thought if I could just go sit by Lake Superior for a while, maybe I could wrap my head around things. For me, it would be the Danube. I live on the flood plains in between the river and its canal, so it's everywhere around here.

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Gods embodied in lakes and rivers
replies.content: Good heavens, yes. All rivers were considered gods in ancient times; the Greeks made offerings to Istros, who was the Danube as a deity -- you can find a coin minted in his honor, and a discussion of river gods generally, here.

comments.title: Praying
comments.content:
My questions:
1) How do you define what praying is?
2) Whats a good praying ritual?

Thank you for being here.

replies.title: Re: Praying
replies.content:
1) Prayer is a conversation with a deity.

2) Depends on the religious tradition you're following and the deity you're working with. If there isn't any specific framework for prayer in your tradition, the first time you pray, light some incense, saying, "I offer this incense to (say the name of the deity here)." Then simply talk to the deity; introduce yourself, tell them why you would like to pray to them, and ask them for guidance in learning how best to pray to them. Then simply spend some time every day praying to the deity, and listen inwardly for the response.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Ahoi,

some probably very basic questions:

1) I have taken up discursive meditation as you had suggested three weeks or so ago. I'm still working on integrating it in my daily routines. Meditating on the same place and time everyday will not work for me, partly for external constraints, partly because in a way I am internally constrained to be flexible, so to say... I meditate while walking (and realized that I have been meditating a lot over the last 20 years or so, without even recognizing it as meditation or having any "framework" for it). Two or three days a week I have the chance to mediate during a break at work in the morning. There is a chance of being disturbed occasionally, either by sounds or by people walking by. Personally, I don't mind this and find no problem maintaining focus on my subject (which generally works surprisingly well) - however there is a chance that somebody might want to talk to me or want something from me... Are there any things to consider if such a case should happen?

2) Divination: I use a Rider/Waite/Smith-Tarot deck. So far I have not cared much about hidden symbolism, numbers, and whatever one could care about. The only thing I look at is what the image means to me and at what "story" or "dream" the cards are telling. So in a way, the cards are speaking my language. Which opens up the possibility, that I could enhance my knowledge in this language. Do you have any suggestions for a good dictionary and how to use it wisely?

Thank you very much, and have a nice Monday!
Nachtgurke

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Everybody has to deal with interruptions to meditation now and again. It's not a problem, and gives you the chance to work on the useful skill of returning to the theme of your meditation when the interruption's done.

2) I don't recommend dictionaries of Tarot meanings, though that may be simply because I've never found them useful. I'd encourage you to learn the language the ordinary way, by having conversations in it with your cards.

comments.title: Vibration
comments.content: When performing the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram, how important is it to be audibly loud enough when attempting to vibrate the deity names? I ask because the best time for me is in my early morning routine while my wife and children are still in bed. I use that time for ritual, meditation, and study. I don't want to make any disturbing noise, which makes me wonder if my whispered attempts get at the desired effect.

replies.title: Re: Vibration
replies.content: The volume is irrelevant; do it as quietly as your circumstances require.  It helps if every so often you can do it at full volume, but I've mostly lived in apartments, and vibrate fairly quietly; I've also done it in what's called "Great Voice," where you say the words silently and "visualize" (or more precisely "tactilize," if that's a word) the buzzing sensation you get from a vibration done right. That works just as well.

comments.title: Talisman as a gift
comments.content:
G'day JMG!

A friend of mine mentioned crafting a talisman as a possible birthday present for his girlfriend. He's not magically inclined, but I happen to have Lon Milo DuQuette's book 'Low Magick' which has instructions on how to build them. The day that he talked to me about this I had come across various mentions of Saturn, so that might pointing towards something. Is it wise and ethically sound to craft such a thing for someone else?

Thanks for your time and generosity!

replies.title: Re: Talisman as a gift
replies.content: First do a divination and find out if it's a good idea; you might also check her horoscope to see if Saturn is favorably placed in it, or at least not under any debility. Other than that, you can certainly craft talismans for other people, so long as you have their permission.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A quick technical question this week: What is the protocol for using planetary talismans in conjunction on a daily basis? I have several at this point (via Chris Warnock) and just want to make sure to avoid counteractions, and even increase synergy if possible. Is it simply a matter of determining whether the planets in question are mutually well-aspected or at least non-aspected in my birth chart, or is a composite chart with that and the relevant talisman's production horoscope necessary? (In particular, my Moon and Mercury talismans are my daily drivers, and both planets are very strong but mutually unaspected in my chart.)

Also, I'm planning to get a bit more into hoodoo and rootwork, and am curious as to the possible interactions, if any, between say Moon-phase-dependent operations and my Moon talisman, which I wear at all times? Do some operations require one to temporarily revert to talismanic neutrality, as it were?

Many thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) As far as I know that isn't a problem, so long as the planets in question aren't in a hostile aspect in your chart.

2) Hoodoo, being natural magic, is pretty flexible when it comes to such things; I've never had a problem with having talismans on my person while doing hoodoo.

comments.title: Pronunciation question
comments.content: In the CGD version of the LRP, Sulw is described as pronounced SILL-w. How is that w at the end pronounced?

replies.title: Re: Pronunciation question
replies.content:
Like "w." ;-)

Say "woof," like a dog. Now draw out the first "w-" sound for a little while before going on to the "-oof." Now say the "w" without the "oof." That's the sound.

comments.title: Parkers' Astrology
comments.content:
Dear John, Is Parkers' Astrology by Julia and Derek Parker any good for beginners? I'm trying to make some sense of my birth chart and learning the very basics and since I have an ex-library copy of the book kicking about thought I'd use it (unless it's a no-no of course...)

Many thanks!
Manuel

replies.title: Re: Parkers' Astrology
replies.content: It's basic but accurate, and ought to do fairly well for a beginner.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

As you know, for many years now I've been working on self development in order to move past abuses from my childhood. Through my recent journaling and meditation, I have come to what I think is the root of the reason why I continue to stay stuck in several negative, self-defeating patterns of living and being. I still have an unhealthy connection to my mother, so that her thoughts and beliefs about how I should act and live supersede my own.

My mom passed away two years ago, and in many ways I haven't dealt with her death. I plan to dedicate this next month to praying and meditating on our relationship, and to communicating with her the best I can so that we can heal and soothe the hurts that still linger. It seems that the season of Samhain is a good time for this.

As part of this healing, and for my own growth, I would like to perform a ritual, on or around the actual date of Samhain, that will help me sever the unhealthy connection that still exists between us. Of course, I would like to do this in a respectful way, too. Is it possible to work with the deceased in this way? And do you have any recommendations on the types of rituals or natural magic workings that will help me with this? I would appreciate any guidance you can give.

As always, you have my sincere thanks and gratitude!

Kelly

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That kind of working is very common, especially in the season of Samhain. One classic way, using the AODA toolkit, is to open a grove in full form, with the Sphere of Protection, calling on each of the elements to bring healing to you and to your mother's spirit, and banishing the unhealthy connection. Then, after the grove is open, light a fire bowl -- this is a cauldron containing a teaspoon each of salt and sugar, with a half cup or so of high-proof alcohol in it, and the cauldron set on a heatproof mat. As it burns, call to mind the memories and burdens and difficulties of the relationship between you, and using your imagination, cast them into the flames to be dissolved back into pure energy and return to the unmanifest. Keep doing this until the last of the flame flickers out, and then close the grove in the usual way. You can also make an amulet from lavender, savory, and vervain leaves in a cloth bag and wear it to help with emotional healing.

replies.title: Asking for a friend known as packshaud
replies.content: This looks like something I could put to good use. What should I change to perform this ritual using CGD methods? I do not intend to do this right now, of course; I need time to prepare.

replies.title: Re: Asking for a friend known as packshaud
replies.content: Do it in an open temple. If you've started working with the Bardic Grade materials, once the temple is open, do the summoning pentagram ritual of Fire and the Calling of Fire; then do the fire bowl ritual as I've described it; then do the License to Depart and the banishing pentagram ritual of Fire, and close in form.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This is perfectly wonderful--thank you!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi,

If I could ask a quick question, to check or confirm something regarding Geomancy interpretation... Working from your book The Art and Practice of Geomancy..

In the chart in question the significator of the quesited passes to the 3rd and 7th houses. The second house is in company capitular with the first house - (which is the significator of the querent), so does the this also count as a conjunction -1 aspect to consider, besides the two other aspects?...
Also, as the significator of the quesited before it passes anywhere is the 4th house, the third is in company simple with it. So is the aspect between the second and third most accurately, or most usefully, though of as a mutation -1?

Hope that makes sense.

Thanks

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Once you bring company of figures into your readings, the complexities do mount up, don't they? It's an interesting question what to call the mode of perfection in a chart when you have figures in company; I tend to call it perfection by company, and leave it at that.

Conjunction in a geomantic chart isn't an aspect, strictly speaking, it's a mode of perfection. Do you have any other mode of perfection in the chart? If not, since company capitular is a fairly weak mode, you've got a tangled situation in which (depending on the testimony of the judge and witnesses) you may or may not get what you want out of it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Firstly, I'd like to thank you for explaining the Cosmic Doctrine to us on ecosophia.net. I don't think I would've understood it without your comments. Linking abstract concepts to concrete practical examples really helps.

I was thinking lately of how summoning and banishing energies is similar to picking and choosing what technologies you use in your life. If you think of it, this concept could be applied to any aspect of a person's life. The idea is simple, but knowing that a person has this kind of power is not trivial. So I wondered if your magical training informed your views on technology to any degree.

Also, what advice would you give to someone who struggles with visualizing the second and third rotations of the Sphere of Protection. Do I need to visualize it from where I'm physically standing or can I see myself from the side surrounded by the rotating sphere (if that makes any sense).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You're most welcome! As for the SoP, no, you need to see yourself inside it. I know it's a challenge, but that's part of the point; you're training your imagination, and that's going to take some heavy lifting from time to time.

comments.title: Backstabbing greedy coworker
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Thanks so much for holding these Q&A sessions. They are very useful and there's always a lot to learn. My question today is about one of my husband's coworkers. My husband has given me his consent to ask you for help. Apologies for the long explanation about office politics.

This colleague of his, was his junior 5 years ago, and in this time he has managed to climb the ladder at light speed. My husband helped him in the beginning, then due to the special relationship between this colleague and a top executive, my husband realised he had to transfer somewhere else so that this guy could have my husband's job - quite a few promotions in a very short time made that possible. My husband managed to transfer out of the direct influence of this exec, however it is still the same business unit, so they work in the same office.

The problem is that after all these promotions this guy doesn't have any idea about how to do his job, my husband has tried to help but he doesn't listen and just gloats about the next promotion he's been promised. He now keeps trying to maneuver things so that somehow my husband gets involved in his project and does his job for him. My husband has picked up on this in several occasions and has told him to take ownership and do his job, in no uncertain terms. The last couple of weeks my husband has been much more blunt than ever, but I can see it's taken a toll on him.

I have reread the Monster's chapter about Vampires and I don't think it's that, so I was wondering if there was something that could be done to protect him from the greedy, manipulative coworker. There is no way to solve this through a normal channels or HR complaint due to high rank of the executive involved.

I have been recovering myself from health issues and only now feel strong enough to start looking for a job, hence the fact that he cannot resign or leave this company at the moment.

Many thanks for your help.

replies.title: Re: Backstabbing greedy coworker
replies.content:
Okay, the first thing you need to do is some serious divination, to find out what's behind the pattern of events. If you know how to read Tarot cards or do some other kind of divination, get to work; if not, see if one of your friends can do that, or go to a professional. One of the reasons that divination is one of the essential skills in magic is that, without it, you're flying blind.

The divination should give you a good idea of how to go about dealing with the situation. In the meantime, your husband may find it useful to take hoodoo cleansing baths at least once a week to clear away the noxious energies that come out of such situations. Run half a tub of water as cold as he can handle it, put in a quarter cup of kosher salt, a quarter cup of vinegar, and a pinch of two of cayenne pepper. Standing in the bath, wash from head to foot, using a clean cloth dipped and wrung out in the water; move the cloth always down from above, never up from below. When he's done and dressed, while the bath drains, he should take some of the water and throw it on the roots of a big strong tree.

comments.title: Heathen Golden Dawn Linguist
comments.content:
Regarding your 'Heathen Golden Dawn' project, if you're not aware of the work of Jackson Crawford you might want to check him out. His translations of the Poetic Edda and the Saga of the Volsungs are very readable. He also has a youtube channel that's worth a look.

https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCXCxNFxw6iq-Mh4uIjYvufg/videos

I know you don't care for visual media personally but hearing an expert on the language and texts speak in old norse might be useful for the project. Most of his videos are in the 10-20 minute range with some exceptions, this one of him reciting the Havamal in old norse in its entirety using reconstructed pronounciation for example:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K3c9QMCFutA

Perhaps you and Sven are aware of the guy but I figured I'd post this to be sure, and to give your heathen readers another resource.

I was also wondering if you had any intention of including a version of each of the rituals in old norse in the final system?

-Jason P.

replies.title: Re: Heathen Golden Dawn Linguist
replies.content:
Um, do you have any idea how many people say to me, "I know you don't care for visual media, but you really have to watch this program"? If I followed their advice I'd be watching jerky little colored shapes on a glass screen for a couple of hours every day, and be bored to death by it. I'll look into Crawford's translations if they're available in print, but I don't do video, full stop, end of sentence.

We don't currently have plans to have the rituals translated into Old Norse since neither Sven nor I are fluent in that language. Nor is it necessarily useful -- none of the people who will be reading and using this book, after all, are living in the tenth century and speaking Old Norse, and the habit of trying to turn Pagan spirituality into a kind of Society for Religious Anachronism, in which people pretend they're living in some other time and place than the one they actually inhabit, has never seemed at all helpful to me.

replies.title: Re: Heathen Golden Dawn Linguist
replies.content:
Jackson Crawford's translation of the Poetic (Elder) Edda seems very good to me in rendering what the impact of the original might have been on the Medieval audience for which it was recited. It was published in 2015 by the Hackett Publishing Co. in Indianapolis.

And his own composition, "The Cowboy Havamal," which he includes at the end is a real tour de force. It takes the form of advice very like that in the original, but put into the mouth of his late grandfather, a cowman from (IIRC) Wyoming, "sad with wisdom and cynical with experience." I've read and reread it over and over.

replies.title: Re: Heathen Golden Dawn Linguist
replies.content: "Society for Religious Ananchronism"--love it. More seriously though, I had a long on-line argument with a friend who has a degree in religious studies and maintained that it is not possible for a person to learn enough of an uncommon language, such as Old Norse for example, to do useful research into the religion. This all came by way of a discussion of the Hard Polytheists, who he regards as self-deluded and dangerously misinformed. I am terrible at learning languages. However I have read of veritable prodigies of self-taught scholarship. One in the Victorian era taught himself Latin by buying old textbooks, later taught himself Hebrew and Sanskrit and eventually become chair of Oriental Languages at Oxford. Surely this sort of thing must be easier in this day of the internet with thousands of texts online at no cost and so forth. So, in your opinion, are attempts to reconstruct religions of which we have only traces left doomed to failure?

replies.title: Re: Heathen Golden Dawn Linguist
replies.content: Not at all. There's a difference between a religion and the language and culture in which that religion was originally expressed; there are a lot of very devout Christians who don't know a word of Aramaic, or for that matter koine Greek. The main difference, though, is that a religion has at least one, and in the case of a polytheist religion more than one, source of information that isn't subject to the vagaries of human language and culture: yes, that would be the god or gods that the religion worships. Since a religion is not a museum piece, but a dance of interaction between gods and human beings, the most effective ways to learn about it are such things as prayer, ritual, and divination -- that is to say, the ways that the religion originally took shape.

comments.title: Magic, Healing and Pain
comments.content:
Hello John Michael Greer

I saw you screen all posts and replies, therefore I did not feel afraid about giving some details or writing at some length. I hope you do not mind reading.

My questions are about healing and pain.

My wife is 69 (a Black American woman, perhaps pertinent astrologically or otherwise) and many of her problems are related to age (I am a white male, half English, half Canadian). We live beside, looking, perhaps a 100 feet above and down, at the ocean, in Europe/Africa.

However, what I would like to know is what I can do to help heal her, or at least ease her pain. Since there is much more she can do if there is not pain.

Pain primarily in the right hip, thigh, knee, groin and perhaps sometimes buttock.

I know neither she nor I am young, but I want to know is, what I can do for her at our age. She has tried various herbs and minerals from online sources, but so far nothing has worked.

I am not sure magic is the way. But if it can work and I can successfully work with it I am willing to work with it.

I have been aware of spirits and presences my whole life. My birth mother, I called my grandmother mom, was perhaps a half baked, half hatched reincarnation of a magical being, who did everyone much harm.

Mostly I have avoided. But after my daughter in law died young, it affected me, I lost my sight in one eye – a few days later, in a third World country (retina detached completely) it has been somewhat repaired), and I began to see (local) spirits and ghosts and presences daily (it has eased off now).

I was not always sure which was which, but the ghosts seemed more obvious, they dressed in local garb, and were sometimes dancing, sometimes drinking, sometimes sitting at tables and rocking chairs, and I think, sometimes dying. The black was not black, especially if I closed my eyes. And when I did, while I was awake, or waking, it seemed all these beings were trying to climb to the edge of my eye, like climbing to the inside edge of a volcano, so they could see out through my closed eye, as you might look out through a volcano opening, though strangely while they were trying to look out through me, they were not in me – it was as if my non functioning eye had become their lens.

My daughter in law was from England, she had always been deliberately thin and on the frail side, but had 3 years before her death become more robust and healthier, about 135 pounds. She then took a trip to England to see her mother, who she had not seen in 20 years. The mother’s claim to fame is “no one can be as sick or in as much pain as she”, and upon my daughter in law’s return it seemed as if an evil competition was started, as to who could be most ill, and therefore deserving of love.

It took 3 years for my daughter in law to die, but it started quickly with her blood jumping up to over 3000 parts xxxx iron, where it should have been only a about a hundred. Sounded like Hemachromatosis but certain specialists said it wasn’t and they would do nothing. You can die from that high a concentration of iron, and the cure is blood letting, but as the medical profession would not do it, my son eventually bought leeches. And the leeches reduced her iron levels to normal.

But that wasn’t the end of her problems or her desire to die, though she said aloud, she wanted to live. My wife and I were living in Nicaragua. We had many times advised them to continue seeing doctors, it is free in Canada, but she did not want to go. (I sent them Kafka’s story “Before the Law” (before the doors, as a caution not to wait and do nothing until it is too late.)

My grandson came to live with us for 7 months. His “single” mother had been far too over-bearing and treating him, and he behaving as a momma’s boy. Though he was trying to extricate himself from this, by getting into trouble, and become a young man. His visit to Nicaragua was for this purpose and succeeded. But on the day he left to return to Canada, his step mother went into the hospital to die 3 months later, and the giant lime tree died, and it rained 2 feet of water and flooded our whole house.

My daughter in law remained dying in the hospital for the next 6 weeks, but then her mother came to visit (planned 3 years earlier), and my daughter in law had herself released from the hospital, to entertain her mother, as if all was normal, and she took her mother sight-seeing and dining, for a week. And then as soon as the mother departed my daughter in law checked back into the hospital and died one month later.

Even, during the entire visit, my daughter in law looked unbelievably 80 years old in pictures – the same as my 80 plus grandmother, when she died. Her mother insisted there was absolutely nothing wrong with her daughter, and that her daughter’s illnesses were absolutely minor, if they existed at all, and did not compare to her own. After the daughter died her father and mother, for some time, refused to believe in the death, and harassed my son for mentioning it on Facebook. Something about it was a libelous false statement that slandered them and led their friends to false conclusions.

I do not know magic, but I was praying for her the whole time. And when she died, I lost my eye and began seeing spirits, and I do think all these events are related.

Since that time I began reading your writings, I have read and am re-reading Dione Fortunes’s “Cosmic Doctrine”, and am currently reading Psychic Self Defence, which makes a lot of sense of my life with my mother, when she was trying to destroy me.

I have been a writer my entire adult life (as a person is a cook), (thought become, action, become habit, become character), everything is almost metaphor for me, therefore Cosmic Doctrine, though not breezy reading, certainly is stimulating much thought, and seems almost literal to me, though you insist it is not. But then for me, all writing is metaphor, in that, as you said, the Story of What Happened is not what happened.

When I was in my young 20’s in University I had been doing much thinking about existence, and we were arguing whether “matter or existence” was random. I argued, if matter was random, it would behave like so many marbles which would never form all the elements and forces, never mind an animal or person, no matter how many eternities passed. There needed to be an organizing will imposing a structure, or rules upon. (Everyone else disagreed) I also argued that if this necessity for an organizing principle were true, then it might be possible for a person, through will, to change the rules locally, or what could be called magic. I did try to find some books, there weren’t many where I lived, and therefore what few I found became like cookbooks of what seemed not very good, or dubious recipes, and something I never did. (Except for a book of First Nation Spells which had a difference about them, and I used those for protection.)

Where I lived as a child till I was 11, was very near the Six Nations Reserve and I was always very aware of what was then called Indian presence. As well Lake Ontario seemed a great Spirit, we saw from the escarpment every day, which escarpment stretching from Niagara Falls north through Tobermory, Manitoulin, then south through Michigan – this escarpment also seemed a Great Spirit, and where we lived was forested in the middle of the city.

What I am wondering today, and asking of you: considering metaphor of the levels in Cosmic Doctrine, the pain and the physical body in one level, with another level above, and another level below.

Is it possible for me, unskilled, never trained in this, to facilitate the movement of the pain and perhaps some degree of the illness, into another level (probably below)(perhaps the rock of this island, the mountains, the cliff we live on and the mountains behind us) – perhaps into something, where it does not hurt my wife, nor injure me (and hopefully anything else), in the process? (I am willing to train myself but because of age there is some need for speed.)

In order that she might feel better, and be happier in so feeling.

We are not looking for wealth, or fame, or acclaim, or recognition.

But my wife and I feeling tolerably well while, and during all the time we are alive, or as long as possible. That would be very nice.

If it helps anything, I seem to have an affinity to plants and mountains, and nature and growing things. I live amongst these, currently overlooking the Atlantic Ocean, on a “European” island off Africa. Some healers I am told use crystals and they also seem to have an affinity to me – I was once given a couple by a female therapist who believed in them, and they did seem to offer protection – though I do not possess any crystals currently.

Thank you for your help and advice.

replies.title: Re: Magic, Healing and Pain
replies.content: It requires as much skill and training to heal pain using magic as it does to heal pain using medicine. If you're willing to devote the next few years to daily practice, you might be able to do something; if that's of interest, I encourage you to pick up any basic handbook of magical training and get to work on it. Barring that, though, I don't really have anything to suggest.

replies.title: Re: Magic, Healing and Pain
replies.content: I apologize for asking.

replies.title: Re: Magic, Healing and Pain
replies.content: Why? You asked the question, you got the answer. If it wasn't the answer you wanted, well, them's the breaks.

replies.title: Re: Magic, Healing and Pain
replies.content: I have experienced a potentially similar hip pain. It was difficult to describe and localize, but the lateral pain was quite sharp and impeded sleeping. No bed was too soft. The cause was my sitting and standing habits and posture. Once I focused on proper posture, stretches, and a little walking, the pain went away.

replies.title: Re: Magic, Healing and Pain
replies.content:
Hi,
Why don't you try praying? You must have some God you like who might be persuaded to help.
Max Rogers

comments.title: C.G. Jung-Bibliothek: Katalog
comments.content:
This is in response to Robert Mathiesen's request last monday (ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/33822.html?thread=2041374#cmt2041374): "There doesn't appear to be even a single copy of this catalog in any academic or public library from which I might borrow it, so as to check whether it has any of Leland's books. Do any of the commentators here have access to a copy?"

If you have access to international inter-library loan services, it may be possible to get a copy from one of the following three Swiss research libraries: the ETH-Bibliothek Zürich, Zentralbibliothek Zürich, and Universitätsbibliothek Basel. The University of Leeds has a copy as well, but for local reference only. As I'm not based in the UK or Switzerland, I can't just go have a look myself, unfortunately.

replies.title: Re: C.G. Jung-Bibliothek: Katalog
replies.content:
Thank you for this. I will indeed ask about Brown U's international inter-library loan services. They have been cutting many corners in library services over the last decade or so, so they may not be able to borrow anything from Europe any longer.

I did hear today from a helpful colleague (F. X. Charet) who does have access to the catalog, and sent me a scan of the page where Leland appears. The only work by Leland that was in Jung's library at the time of his death was the first edition of Leland's "Aradia; or, the Gospel of the Witches" (1899).

The first edition of "Aradia" never circulated very widely, so I am fascinated to see that Jung had a copy.

comments.title: Hermetics and personality
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've always been interested in Hermeticism but one of the things that has, in the past, kept me away from making an earnest go at it was the other people I've known who have been involved in it. I'm talking about people I've known personally; while from my reading there appear to be people out there who are relatively sane and stable who are involved in this kind of thing, the ones I've known personally who have been involved in explicitly Hermetic currents of practice (OTO and GD) almost without exception narcissistic drug addicts. I expect this is a result of my very small sample size but I wonder if there is something about this work that encourages those tendencies in people who are predisposed to them.

I remember you mentioning a few weeks ago that there are certain personality types who ought to avoid New Thought work. Do you think the same thing applies to other kinds of magical work?

thanks!

replies.title: Re: Hermetics and personality
replies.content: Yes, very much so. I can't speak to the OTO tradition, since I don't practice it, but Golden Dawn magic is really dangerous for people who have the kind of unstable ego that's vulnerable to what Jung called inflation. The focus on solar and Tiphareth symbolism unbalances such people all too readily, and they turn into the classic type of the failed Golden Dawn occultist: i.e., clones of Aleister Crowley, or as you neatly summed it up, narcissistic drug addicts. If you're going to do the Golden Dawn work you need to have an ego strong and stable enough that you don't particularly care what other people think of you; you need to have good healthy relationships with people who are close to you and will tell you if you're getting out of line; and it really helps if you also have a stable religious life, and so are used to the fact that there are beings in the cosmos who are far mightier, wiser, and better than you will ever be.

replies.title: Re: Hermetics and personality
replies.content:
(Not the original poster)

This is fascinating! May I ask if there are similar personality contraindications concerning the magic in your Druid Magic Handbook, and, if so, what these contraindications may be?

replies.title: Re: Hermetics and personality
replies.content:
That's a good question, to which I don't yet have a good answer. The system of magic in that book hasn't been in circulation long enough for me to encounter anybody who's crashed and burned using it. The failed Druids I've encountered have been Popes with a congregation of one: people who are in love with the idea of "being a Druid" because they think it means other people will look up adoringly at them and listen with bated breath to their pronouncements of wisdom. Such people rarely take up magical practice, though, because it doesn't fulfill their goal of getting people to worship them, and if they do get into magic they normally quit as soon as they discover that they actually do have to start at the beginning with simple practices, rather than simply putting on a costume and basking in everyone else's admiration.

So we'll just have to see what kind of personality gets in trouble with The Druid Magic Handbook. I tried to set it up to be as foolproof as possible, but I know that all that happens when you make something foolproof is that someone invents a better fool.

replies.title: Re: Hermetics and personality
replies.content: Thank you! What I've been getting interested in lately is the work of Denning and Philips, the Aurum Solis material, which is also has a very Solar focus, so this is helpful to understand.

replies.title: Re: Hermetics and personality
replies.content: In my experience, the Aurum Solis material is very similar to the GD in its potentials for imbalance, though it doesn't seem quite as attractive to narcissists.

replies.title: Re: Hermetics and personality
replies.content: I was involved in the OTO for several years during the 1980s, when Grady McMurtry had been working on reviving it. During that period, when things were still wild and wooly, I think your observations applied to the order and the people who were initiates of it.

comments.title: An Unwanted Connection
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I was wondering if you could give me some advice on a sticky situation I got myself into when I was young and ignorant (I am now a few years older, and marginally less ignorant). Sorry for the long-windedness.
When I was in college, I spent a few weeks hanging out with a guy who was weirdly charismatic, but who turned out to be a first-class creep. My intuition was pinging me with a feeling that I later learned to recognize as “creep alert, take cover,” but at the time mistook it for an unfortunate combination of repressed racism and social anxiety, and ignored it. I was feeling increasingly besieged in the guy’s company, and ultimately he tried to grab and kiss me and I was so grossed out that I fled, started avoiding him, and was almost immediately flabbergasted that I’d ever given him the time of day. All our mutual acquaintances hated him.
Here’s the problem. Before I came to my senses, I gifted this guy with an old hat of mine that I no longer wore, but that I had worn almost daily for a few years, to the point that it sort of became a part of my identity for a while. I instantly regretted giving it away, but assumed I’d get over it. Instead, for years after parting ways with this fellow, I’ve felt uncomfortably connected with him. The vivid memory of him will pop up randomly in my head, accompanied by a feeling like he’s reaching for me, looking at me, or touching me.
I’ve since learned that a person’s hair, clothing, etc. can be used in magical workings concerning them, because those objects remain connected to them. My question is, is there any way to break my connection with this object/person? Or take back whatever immaterial aspect of the hat I’m connected to? I’ve tried to contact him, hoping to get the object itself back, but his number no longer works. I did try the hoodoo bath you recommended to another reader, and that did seem to help. I’m still very much a beginner with this stuff, so I apologize if I’ve overlooked some basic principle or something. I would really appreciate your insight!

Marie K

P.S. Also, a data point for you: I’ve had straight pins on or around pretty much every screen in the house since you first recommended the practice. Our household television consumption has gone from very little to almost none. My computer usage actually went up for a while, though, because it no longer made me feel so groggy and ill to use the thing. Unfortunately I had to take the pin off the laptop after a few weeks, because the poor machine was acting up, and I can’t afford to replace it right now.

replies.title: Re: An Unwanted Connection
replies.content:
It sounds as though the person in question may have been using magic on you in the first place -- the kind of attraction where you look back and have no idea what made you spend time with him in the first place can be a sign that somebody was using magic on you. What you need is called a "cut and clear" spell. One I'd suggest is to get a small glass jar, a bottle of cayenne pepper, and a square piece of paper a few inches across torn from a brown paper bag -- it should be torn, not cut, on each side. Write the person's name five times on it:

Joe Blow
Joe Blow
Joe Blow
Joe Blow
Joe Blow

Then turn the paper 90 degrees, and write five times across the name

Cut and clear
Cut and clear
Cut and clear
Cut and clear
Cut and clear

It should end up looking like a grid, with the name written one way cut across by the words written the other.

Fold the paper away from you, then turn it 90 degrees and fold it away from you again. Then put it in the jar, fill the jar with cayenne pepper, take it to a river or creek, and throw it in.

Afterwards, take a hoodoo cleansing bath -- the instructions are further up in this week's comments, in response to "Nasty Backstabbing Coworker." Repeat the bath any time you feel the person is trying to resume the connection. It shouldn't take many repeats before it's over and done with.

replies.title: Re: An Unwanted Connection
replies.content: What do you do if you live somewhere all the creeks are dry? Can you throw the bottle into a dry creekbed, directly into the ocean, or does something else work? Could you bury it in the earth?

replies.title: Re: An Unwanted Connection
replies.content: Take it to the ocean. It has to be moving water.

replies.title: Re: An Unwanted Connection
replies.content: Thank you!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
In fantasy fiction there is the concept of the "familiar" of a magician, in the form of a cat or a frog etc What is the reality behind this, according to the occult lore?

Concerning Astrology, it has occured to me that if the planets stand, among other things, as the parts of the self, then the aspects can be read as the relations inside the self, and can show where we completely lack control, where we have control but in a dificult manner, what we conduct naturally, what goes together for good or ill etc

Thus for example my saturn moon conjunction shows that my personality has a tendency to yield to my habits, fears and mental structures but the sextil to saturn from mars conjunct sun shows that is posible to consciously work on those structures.

I know, its a bit general, but i would like to know if this aproach seems useful to you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The term was originally "familiar spirit," and the reality is simply that people can establish relationships with spirits, some of whom can take animal form on the astral plane.

2) Good. That's one of the basic tools of psychological astrology.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You might want to read Cunning Folk and Familiar Spirits by Emma Wilby. The following text was copied from the backcover:

This book examines the folkloric roots of familiar lore in early modern Britain from historical, anthropological, and comparative religious perspectives. It argues that beliefs about witches’ familiars were rooted in beliefs surrounding the use of fairy familiars by beneficent magical practitioners or 'cunning folk,' and corroborates this through a comparative analysis of familiar beliefs found in traditional Native American and Siberian shamanism. The author then goes on to explore the experiential dimension of familiar lore by drawing parallels between early modern familiar encounters and visionary mysticism as it appears in both tribal shamanism and medieval European contemplative traditions. These perspectives challenge the reductionist view of popular magic in early modern Britain often presented by historians.

comments.title: Anthroposofic chart, Writing practice, Anti-glamour
comments.content:
JMG - Thank you for all your time and work! Several questions:
1. What is a 'Anthroposofic Chart'? While looking for unrelated antique prints for sale I came across this 1918 chart made by the Societas Rosicruciana, with the description relating it to 'Alchemy and Religious Golden Dawn'. The photos weren't detailed enough.
2. How is keeping a journal to work through difficulties (say, fear of making decisions) different from establishing a writing habit (say, for writing stories)? Should one of these activities be done to the exclusion of the other? In other words, work through the fear (or whatever) journal before starting on the other writing work? Or, simply keep separate notebooks?
-PatriciaT
3. Re: Anti-Glamour exercise. First off, I would not have a TV, as any value it might have is far outweighed by its time-wasting and other negative effects. But (sigh) Significant Other MUST have his TV!. Anyway, the anti-glamour measure (shape needle) still works to some degree, but the effects appear to be waning. I wonder if there is a way to renew its working somehow.

replies.title: Re: Anthroposofic chart, Writing practice, Anti-glamour
replies.content:
1) Hmm! "Anthroposophy" is wisdom dealing with the human soul and subtle bodies. Did you buy the poster? If not, do you happen to know if it's still for sale?

2) They can certainly be done by the same person, but use different notebooks and do it at different times. Working on a journal to heal your psyche, though, will help you develop your writing skills!

3) Check to see if the needle's tarnished. You may need to replace it.

replies.title: Re: Anthroposofic chart, Writing practice, Anti-glamour
replies.content:
Not really for publication, and probably you already got this from the OP, but:

http://sturgisantiques.com/1918_anthroposofic_chart_by_frater_wade_for_societas_rosicruciana_in_americ

Bogatyr

replies.title: Re: Anthroposofic chart, Writing practice, Anti-glamour
replies.content:
https://sturgisantiques.com/1918_anthroposofic_chart_by_frater_wade_for_societas_rosicruciana_in_americ


https://www.etsy.com/listing/631114589/1918-anthroposofic-chart-for-societas

replies.title: Re: Anthroposofic chart,
replies.content:
re 'Anthroposofic Chart' it is at this moment for sale on Etsy as "1918 Anthroposofic Chart for Societas Rosicruciana 22 x 31 Inches Alchemy and Religious Golden Dawn". I was just curious more in general interest sort of way. It is also way out of my price range ($395.00).

Thank you for your answers

replies.title: Re: Anthroposofic chart,
replies.content: Fascinating. It's a visual chart of the theology and cosmology of early 20th century American Rosicrucianism -- you can find basically the same material in Max Heindel's book The Rosicrucian Cosmo-Conception, though not in the same neatly tabulated form.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Are there any Welsh sea/river gods? I am studying the DMH and I get that it makes sense to stick with one pantheon. But the sea is a big part of my life, so I figured I would check!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good heavens, yes. Llyr, Nodens, and Dylan Eil Ton are all sea deities. Among old Celtic deities of the sort also invoked in the DMH, Grannus, Nantosuelta and Nodens are all water gods.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear jmg
Since Kabbalah is part of the golden dawn does that mean it’s ok to use the meditations, prayers and other spiritual exercises from Jewish Kabbalah?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not really. The Cabala wasn't originally Jewish, you know -- it seems to have originated in the Hellenistic world out of Middle Platonism sometime around the beginning of the Common Era -- and was picked up by the Gnostics not long thereafter; the classical writers Celsus and Origen discuss the Tree of Life diagram in their writings, and a version of the Tree of Life also appears in Chinese occultism in the tenth century, one of many things carried along the Silk Road. (Alchemy seems to have arrived in the Western world from China in the same way, though a little earlier). The first Jewish Cabalistic writings date from rabbinic circles in southern France in the eleventh century.

The Jewish branch of the Cabala is a very rich, potent, and thoroughly developed system of philosophy and practice, and if you happen to be Jewish by birth or conversion, I'd encourage you to take it up if you feel called to it. The Hermetic branch of the Cabala is not the same thing, though -- it differs from the Jewish branch roughly the same way that the German language differs from English, via the same process of growing apart over the centuries -- and you can't just plug-and-play parts from one system into the other. In particular, if you're not Jewish, a lot of the material in the Jewish branch of the Cabala isn't going to work for you at all, because you don't have the contacts and connections with the god of Israel and his angels that a practicing Jew has, and those are essential to the proper functioning of that branch of the Cabala.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Jewish Cabala was developed by and for men, as in males, and it might be unwise to practice it if one is a women. I remember stern warnings of rabbis against a singer called Madonna who wanted to practice it; they said it would drive her mad. I do not know how much of this would be by accident or design.

Besides, it would be wiser to pick pieces of it only if you know what you are doing—forgive me for being blunt, but if you are asking, then you should not be doing it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Well, I don't know how much Madonna practiced, but she doesn't seem any crazier than she was when she started.

- S. T. Silva.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Is exhaustion a usual side effect of starting spiritual practices?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No. What practices have you started?

comments.title: Brain Wave music
comments.content:
Hi JMG, thank you for doing your Magic Mondays. I've been reading your blogs and buying your books for years, but this is the first time I've posted. I've begun to pursue magical training, but am at the very, very beginning.

For over a year now, I've used brain wave music to help me focus while writing. I have used this music regularly, sometimes daily, for an hour or more. The music uses certain frequencies and rhythms to 'entrain' the brain to focus, specifically, beta-rate modulation.

It occurred to me, with your previous warnings about mixing eastern and western approaches to energy work, to wonder whether I should be concerned about this musical 'training' I've been engaging in.

Maybe one does not have anything to do to another--I am that much of a beginner that I really have no idea. I am hoping to get your thoughts on this. Should I be worried whether this focus music will conflict with the energy work I am pursuing?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Brain Wave music
replies.content: I have no idea how those will interact; I've never encountered anyone who's tried the combination. One of the complexities of this work is that we're all basically doing experiments on our own brains, and tolerably often there's no previously gathered data to guide us!

comments.title: God is that you?
comments.content: How can we know if the god we have in mind is the one who is responding to a prayer and not another sprit? What about if it is your first contact with that god?

replies.title: Re: God is that you?
replies.content: You can't. That's what faith is about.

comments.title: Witch Ball
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

At the end of one of Lucky Mojo’s recent radio shows on protection spells, a free spell was given, as is the custom, at the end of the program. It features a small cobalt blue bottle, three devils shoestrings, a pinch of agrimony and salt, all wrapped in a paper on which is written the Lords Prayer, and/or Psalm 37:15, and/or the 6th Seal of Mars. Said paper was then tied closed with red thread, put in the jar, and good to go.

Since it is Magic Monday, may I please ask:

1) Is there any benefit to assembling the bits and pieces at a specific time? If so, may I ask what that time would be?

2) Must it sit near a door or window, or would any location work just fine?

3) Should it be shaken every now and then, to keep it happy? Any care and feeding advice?

And lastly, if one witch ball is good, would a second one in, say a bedroom, be better?

Thanks so much!
OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Witch Ball
replies.content: One good thing about Lucky Mojo is that when they give you a spell, they pretty reliably give you all the details you need. Ergo, if the spell didn't specify a time, you can do it whenever -- this is true of many hoodoo workings; if the spell doesn't specify care and feeding, it doesn't need that; and if the spell says put it by a door or window, that's what you need to do. As for a second, I'd say try one for a while and see what the results are before going on.

comments.title: Comment rather than question
comments.content:
I’ve no question at the moment, just a comment. Lately I’ve been following an excellent series of videos put out by the Swedenborg Foundation exploring that mystic writer’s ideas in considerable depth. It’s fascinating stuff, all to do with the nature of the spiritual world and our connections with it. Though Swedenborg was Christian and a devout Lutheran whereas my own worldview tends toward paganism and Hermeticism, I find this really doesn’t detract appreciably from the value & interest of his ideas. It’s worth checking out.

Kevin

P.S. - I like the photo of L. Frank Baum. I may check out some of his works to see if I can spot the magical content. K.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Comment rather than question
replies.content: Swedenborg was Lutheran, but he was very much on the magical end of the Lutheran movement -- yes, there was such a thing; the original Rosicrucians and Jakob Boehme were other examples -- and that end of Lutheranism had very close connections to Renaissance Hermeticism. He's not as popular now as he once was, but in the late 18th and early 19th centuries a lot of people who were into the occult went to Swedenborgian churches.

comments.title: egregores and tulpas
comments.content:
I noticed that “egregore” featured in some of the comments in this round of Magic Monday, so I decided to risk bringing this up…

There is a fellow, who I stumbled across a while back — Joshua P. Warren — and I didn’t know what to make of him, although I suppose he could be called an occult operator in some loose sense. I first came across him when he appeared on an episode of George Noory’s Coast to Coast program (on which you’ve appeared as well). In case you haven’t heard of him, he’s been involved in such things as para-cymatics, the creation of customized sigils, runs the Haunted Asheville Ghost Tours, and manifests various kinds of tulpas… so, he’s thoroughly invested in experiencing “things weird.”

In any case, there was an incident in which he created a kind of tulpa or ghost, based on a previous experiment which was conducted as “research” for a Warner Bros. film called The Apparition, a film the producers said was based on the premise that “paranormal events happen because people believe in them,” and they fictionalized a back story of someone who did an experiment which summoned an “inhuman entity.”

Please bear with me, as the next para is a quote: “Warren reported on his own attempt at creating a ghost using a lab setup, pointing out that "this actually worked." Components of the original Phillip experiment were combined with technology that amplified brain waves, he disclosed. The subject was asked to focus on a tiki doll and her thoughts were broadcast through equipment that magnified them into the equivalent of thousands of people thinking the same thing, Warren continued. Eventually, a huge harmonic-type field built up which damaged the equipment and ended the experiment, he recalled. According to Warren, seven days later he began to hear scratching sounds in the walls and saw a distorted human-shaped shadow walk down his staircase. In addition, objects were found inexplicably strewn about, the electrical system in Warren's building melted down, and other tenants claimed to have seen a shadowy being, he said.”

I am wondering how something like this relates to the idea of an egregore, which seems based on a much larger field of psychic energy (and in the case of the zetigeist example, more mixed range of creative “input”), as well as a possible relation to projection as Jung understood it. Warren’s case is very specific, and however, there was some (apparent) technological application involved, which then actually produced an entity. I recall Warren relating his story to Noory on that program, saying that the “entity” is still appearing in house where it took place, and that no one can live there as a result. It was all relayed in a rather lighthearted, blithe manner, and made me question the ethics of such actions.

Wonder what your thoughts are on any of this…

many thanks,

Petrus

replies.title: Re: egregores and tulpas
replies.content:
I've never been sure what to make of such people. It's like those supposedly true stories of magical workings in Kenneth Grant's books where the result of the spell is that everyone who participated went insane and two of them were devoured by dogs.

You can certainly create what occultists call an artificial elemental, if you want to; it's a common practice in some schools of magic, and it can be done in a variety of ways, most of them less theatrical than the one Warren apparently used. I don't use that method -- to my mind, it's easier and less tiring to work with natural elementals, spirits, and intelligences, and you're also much less likely to end up with a situation like the one Warren has.

replies.title: Re: egregores and tulpas
replies.content:
Thanks JMG.
I'm totally in agreement with respect to working with nature spirits and elementals, and I'm sometimes fascinated by people like Marko Pogacnik who has been doing that for many years, and has even developed his own kind of geomantic "Gaia-acupuncture" methods... On the other hand, Warren seems into it for the kicks, projecting a kind of childish prurience which seems irresponsible...

Thanks again,
Petrus
